speaker: DEV
text: Every so often you run into an idea that makes you rethink something you'd basically stopped questioning. Homework is definitely one of those for me. Hadn't thought about it in years — then I heard what my guest today's been building, and now I can't stop turning it over.

speaker: DEV
text: Welcome to Second Look. I'm Dev.

speaker: DEV
text: My guest today is Alex — she's been rethinking what homework actually is. Alex, thanks for coming on.

speaker: ALEX
text: Thanks for having me. But fair warning, I'm still getting used to talking about this out loud.

speaker: DEV
text: That's always my favorite type of guest.

speaker: DEV
text: So set the scene for me. What's actually going on with homework in your view?

speaker: ALEX
text: Well, it still gets assigned, still gets reluctantly completed and handed in, everything looks fine on paper. But what's missing is any signal in between — a kid turns something in, and weeks later a test shows half the class missed the same idea.

speaker: DEV
text: And that could even be the first anyone's hearing about it.

speaker: ALEX
text: Usually, yeah. Homework tells you somebody filled in the blanks, but it doesn't always tell you whether they understood what they were filling in.

speaker: DEV
text: Huh.

speaker: DEV
text: So what'd you actually build?

speaker: ALEX
text: Homework, but made the way a good podcast is made. Real pacing, real curiosity — not just someone reading a textbook out loud, or a stale, unstructured interview. Then a short quiz at the end to check what actually landed. And the results go back to the teacher — not a grade, but who finished it, and what tripped people up.

speaker: DEV
text: ...Okay.

speaker: DEV
text: How did you even land on this idea?

speaker: ALEX
text: Honestly? Watching my niece. She'll get through four episodes of some show back to back, no one making her — and then groan about twenty minutes of reading for school.

speaker: DEV
text: Sure.

speaker: ALEX
text: And I kept thinking — what if homework showed up the way a new episode does? Something she's actually a little curious to open, instead of something she's just told to do.

speaker: DEV
text: Huh. That's a genuinely different question than "how do we make homework easier."

speaker: ALEX
text: ...and that's the part I'm actually excited about, yeah.

speaker: DEV
text: Why though?

speaker: ALEX
text: Because I'm not going to sit here and promise every kid loves their homework now. But if it's something they can actually experience — maybe even enjoy — two people talking, with real curiosity in it — that's quite different from a worksheet.

speaker: DEV
text: So the win condition isn't just — "did they finish it."

speaker: ALEX
text: yeah... it's — "did they want to."

speaker: DEV
text: Can I bring something to this? I did a little digging before this talk.

speaker: ALEX
text: Please.

speaker: DEV
text: So — everyone talks about test scores when this comes up. But the real cost shows up after — once a kid's already behind, and someone has to catch them up.

speaker: ALEX
text: The tutoring, yeah.

speaker: DEV
text: Right. Ontario alone put something like one hundred seventy-five million dollars into expanding tutoring — on top of a separate chunk just for a dedicated tutoring program. The UK's national tutoring effort started with something like one billion pounds in catch-up money. In the US, states set aside somewhere around four billion dollars in relief funding specifically for tutoring.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yeah — the actual cost of finding out too late.

speaker: DEV
text: Billions. And that's before you count a teacher's own prep time, or a parent trying to help with material they haven't touched since they were a kid.

speaker: ALEX
text: Which is a real chunk of who this is actually for. Parents who want to help and just don't remember the content anymore.

speaker: DEV
text: So if you catch the confusion in week two instead of month three? —

speaker: ALEX
text: yeah — the money's already budgeted. It's just landing much later than it needs to.

speaker: DEV
text: So — who actually benefits from something like this.

speaker: ALEX
text: Well, students, primarily. Parents, we just covered.

speaker: DEV
text: Teachers.

speaker: ALEX
text: Teachers, yeah. They already know their kids well. The problem's just that the proof — which idea actually tripped up the room — is buried in a pile of grading that takes real time to get through — this gets it to them while there's still time to course correct.

speaker: DEV
text: Can I push on that? Because anything that shows "which questions a class missed" — depending who's holding that information, that can start to feel aimed at the teacher instead of for them.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yeah — I take that seriously, genuinely. Every bit of this is aimed at what a class understood, not who taught it or how. And nobody's required to check it — it's there if a teacher wants it, not one more login demanding attention.

speaker: DEV
text: So it's a heads-up? Optional? Nobody's grading the teacher on it?

speaker: ALEX
text: That's the shape of it, yeah. And honestly, it should save time, not add to the pile — fewer surprises means less scrambling to reteach something the week before a test.

speaker: DEV
text: And a school board looks at the same information and sees... what exactly?

speaker: ALEX
text: Which concepts aren't landing, across a whole grade, before it shows up on a provincial assessment. That moves it out of just one classroom — which can help future planning, for a whole grade.

speaker: DEV
text: Which is probably the version a Ministry actually wants to see. Not "kids like it more" — but "here's where the gaps are, earlier than you'd otherwise know."

speaker: ALEX
text: Uh... can I bring something up? It's not fully built yet, but it's on the roadmap, and I think about it a lot.

speaker: DEV
text: Go for it.

speaker: ALEX
text: Long breaks. Summer's the big one — kids lose real ground over it. It's one of the more consistently replicated findings in education research — going back decades. A chunk of learning, just gone, by September.

speaker: DEV
text: Right — and nothing's really checking in during that stretch.

speaker: ALEX
text: Nothing structured, no. So — same format, same short episodes, aimed at a different six weeks. Not schoolwork. More like staying warm.

speaker: DEV
text: Staying warm. Huh — I like that.

speaker: ALEX
text: Again, this is not fully built out yet — I want to be upfront about that. But there's a second piece of it I like even more: using it before the next school year starts. Short episodes on what's coming in September, so a kid walks in already having heard the shape of it at least once.

speaker: DEV
text: Yeah — instead of it just landing on them cold in week one.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. I know I would've liked that. Just — softening the landing a little.

speaker: DEV
text: That whole summer stretch — the weeks that do the most damage are also the weeks nobody's paying attention to.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's the whole idea.

speaker: DEV
text: So where's this actually at right now. Idea, or does it exist?

speaker: ALEX
text: It exists. We built a full example in this exact format — real audio, a real quiz at the end — for an actual eighth-grade science topic.

speaker: DEV
text: Actually built, or is this the part where it turns out to be a mockup?

speaker: ALEX
text: Real. I keep bracing for that question — I think people expect a slide deck by now.

speaker: DEV
text: Fair enough.

speaker: ALEX
text: There's a link right below this, whenever you want to hear it. About eight minutes.

speaker: DEV
text: I think I'll give it a listen tonight.

speaker: ALEX
text: Good. Let me know what you think.

speaker: DEV
text: Alex, thanks for coming on.

speaker: ALEX
text: Thanks for having me.
